title: Bread Sauce
subtitle: A thick, creamy infused milk sauce, the classic companion to roast chicken and game
excerpt: Bread sauce is milk infused with onion, cloves, and bay, thickened with white breadcrumbs until it has the consistency of a loose porridge. It is a medieval sauce that has survived because it does something no other sauce does: it is simultaneously mild and deeply aromatic, with the clove-and-bay infusion making the whole thing more interesting than the sum of its parts. It must be made fresh and served hot.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: bread sauce recipe with onion and cloves. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg #35075: bread sauce method. Classic British roast bird accompaniment dating from the medieval period.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the infused milk base up to 1 hour ahead. Add the breadcrumbs and finish just before serving, as the sauce thickens on standing and may need a little more hot milk to loosen.

glossaryTerms.definition: A technique from classical and British cookery in which whole cloves are pushed into a halved or whole onion, then the onion is simmered in milk to infuse it with both the onion's sweetness and the clove's warm spice. The onion is discarded before serving.

content.text: The flavour of bread sauce comes almost entirely from the infusion, not the bread. Using a

simmered gently in milk for 20 minutes gives the sauce its character. The bread is simply the thickener. Use white bread without the crusts; brown bread makes the sauce grey and gives an earthy flavour that competes with the spice infusion.

items.prepNote: pushed into the cut face of the onion halves
crusts removed, torn into small pieces

content.text: Put {{whole-milk}} of milk, the clove-studded {{onion}} onion halves, and {{bay-leaves}} in a small saucepan. Bring to the barest simmer over a low heat. Do not boil. Keep at a very gentle simmer for 20 minutes to infuse. Remove the onion and bay leaves.

Add the {{white-bread}} torn bread pieces to the hot infused milk. Stir over a low heat for 5 minutes until the bread has absorbed the milk and the sauce is thick and smooth. Stir in {{unsalted-butter}} of butter, {{sea-salt-flakes}}, {{nutmeg}}, and the {{double-cream}} of cream if using. Rest for 10 minutes before serving; the sauce continues to thicken as it sits. If it becomes too thick, stir in a splash of warm milk.

rows.cause: Bread absorbed more milk than expected, or sauce cooked too long
rows.fix: Add warm milk a tablespoon at a time, stirring between additions, until the sauce reaches a loose, creamy consistency.

rows.cause: Infusion time too short or cloves not fresh
rows.fix: Infuse for the full 20 minutes. Check that the cloves are still aromatic: old whole cloves lose their volatile oils and barely flavour the milk.

content.text: Bread sauce is one of the oldest surviving British sauces. Its origins are medieval, from a period when bread-thickened sauces were standard rather than unusual. It has outlasted most of that tradition because it suits roast chicken and game birds in a way nothing else does: the mild, creamy sauce with its clove undertone balances the rich, dark meat without competing with it.
